code:
def generate_signals(df, params):
    import pandas as pd
    import numpy as np
    import ta
    lookback = params['lookback']
    adx_threshold = params['adx_threshold']
    
    # Compute Donchian channels (previous period high/low)
    donchian_high = df['high'].rolling(window=lookback).max().shift(1)
    donchian_low = df['low'].rolling(window=lookback).min().shift(1)
    
    # Compute ADX
    adx_indicator = ta.trend.ADXIndicator(high=df['high'], low=df['low'], close=df['close'], window=14)
    adx = adx_indicator.adx()
    
    # Entry signals
    long_entry = (df['close'] > donchian_high) & (adx > adx_threshold)
    short_entry = (df['close'] < donchian_low) & (adx > adx_threshold)
    
    # Combine: long=1, short=-1, neutral=0
    signals = pd.Series(0, index=df.index)
    signals[long_entry] = 1
    signals[short_entry] = -1
    
    # Exit: hold until opposite signal (implicit via signal change) — no explicit exit needed
    # Ensure integer type
    return signals.astype(int)
rationale: GBP/JPY tends to trend strongly after breaking out of a Donchian channel when ADX indicates a strong trend, capturing momentum while filtering false breakouts.

self_critique.why_this_might_be_noise: Breakouts can be whipsawed in choppy markets; ADX is lagging and may confirm too late.
self_critique.what_would_disprove_this: If GBP/JPY mean-reverts after breakouts or ADX fails to distinguish trends in this volatile pair.
